event: Hunter-gatherer communities related to Pygmy peoples inhabit the equatorial forests and savannas of the region during the Neolithic era.
Megalithic monuments are erected at Bouar in the western highlands, marking some of Central Africa's oldest monumental architecture.
Bantu-speaking agriculturalists begin migrating into the region, spreading farming and ironworking technologies.
Ironworking communities expand across the savanna, supporting larger settled populations and increased trade.
Various Adamawa-Ubangi and Bantu-speaking peoples establish farming villages and chiefdoms across the region.
Trade networks connect the region to the Sudanic kingdoms and trans-Saharan routes through goods such as ivory and slaves.
The sultanates and kingdoms to the north and east begin conducting slave raids into the territory of the future CAR.
The Bandia-led Nzakara and Zande states emerge in the southeast as organized polities.
The Azande establish powerful kingdoms in the southeast under the Avongara aristocracy.
Intensified slave raiding by Sudanese, Bornu, and Wadai traders devastates and depopulates much of the region.
Sultanates such as Bangassou, Rafai, and Zemio consolidate power along the Ubangi River trade routes.
The Berlin Conference assigns the region to the French sphere of influence during the Scramble for Africa.
France and the Congo Free State agree on the Ubangi River as a boundary, beginning French claims to the area.
The French establish a trading post at Bangui on the Ubangi River, founding the future capital.
France formally creates the colony of Ubangi-Shari (Oubangui-Chari) to administer the territory.
France grants vast land concessions to private companies, ushering in an era of brutal exploitation and forced labor.
Ubangi-Shari is administratively joined with Chad under a single French governance structure.
Ubangi-Shari becomes part of the federation of French Equatorial Africa, with its capital eventually at Brazzaville.
The Kongo-Wara rebellion, also called the War of the Hoe Handle, erupts against forced labor and colonial taxation.
French forces suppress the Kongo-Wara rebellion after several years of widespread resistance.
Ubangi-Shari gains representation in the French Union, and Barthelemy Boganda is elected to the French National Assembly.
Barthelemy Boganda founds the Movement for the Social Evolution of Black Africa (MESAN) to advance African interests.
The territory becomes the autonomous Central African Republic within the French Community, with Boganda as its first leader.
Barthelemy Boganda, the nation's founding father, dies in a plane crash shortly before independence.
The Central African Republic gains full independence from France on 13 August, with David Dacko as first president.
The Central African Republic is admitted to the United Nations on 20 September.
President David Dacko establishes a one-party state under MESAN.
Colonel Jean-Bedel Bokassa seizes power in a New Year's Eve coup, overthrowing David Dacko.
Bokassa declares himself president for life.
Bokassa proclaims the Central African Empire and crowns himself Emperor Bokassa I.
Bokassa stages a lavish, costly coronation ceremony modeled on Napoleon, draining the national treasury.
Soldiers massacre schoolchildren protesting compulsory uniforms, sparking international outrage against Bokassa.
France backs Operation Barracuda, restoring David Dacko to power and ending the empire.
General Andre Kolingba seizes power in a bloodless coup, ousting Dacko.
Bokassa returns from exile and is tried and convicted of murder and other crimes.
Ange-Felix Patasse wins the first multiparty presidential elections, ending military rule.
A series of army mutinies over unpaid wages destabilizes the government and require foreign intervention.
African peacekeepers under the MISAB mission deploy to restore order after the mutinies.
The UN deploys the MINURCA peacekeeping mission to stabilize the country.
Francois Bozize seizes power in a coup while President Patasse is abroad.
Francois Bozize wins presidential elections, legitimizing his rule.
Peace agreements are signed with northern rebel groups amid ongoing instability.
The Seleka rebel coalition launches an offensive against the Bozize government.
Seleka rebels capture Bangui and Michel Djotodia becomes the first Muslim president, triggering sectarian violence.
Djotodia resigns amid escalating conflict between Seleka and anti-balaka militias, and Catherine Samba-Panza becomes interim president.
The UN establishes the MINUSCA peacekeeping mission to protect civilians during the civil war.
Faustin-Archange Touadera wins the presidential election, returning the country to constitutional rule.
The government and fourteen armed groups sign a peace agreement in Khartoum to end the civil war.
Rebel groups launch a major offensive ahead of elections, attempting to march on Bangui.
Voters approve a new constitution allowing President Touadera to seek additional terms, amid concerns over Russian Wagner Group influence.
